paragraphs.body: Dialogicious

annotations.comment: I love that Snoop Dog album. ;)

paragraphs.body: I have found throughout this class that good dialogue is really hard to write. My first short story has limited dialogue so for my second short story I decided to experiment with more dialogue to create a conflict. It is definitely not perfect dialogue and could have probably been written entirely differently but I don’t think Anne Lamott would say that it reads like something from a childhood play by the Gabor sisters. I will look at the use of dialogue in Kennedy from “Best American Short Stories of 2020” I will also look at what Anne Lamott says in Bird by Bird. Then I will compare to the dialogue written in both of my short stories.

Since dialogue can create conflict, setting and plot it is essential to any good story. However, it can’t be thrown in randomly it has to be used with purpose and sound natural. Dialogue is capable of revealing what the characters wish to share or conceal. In my first short story, I utilized dialogue to contrast with the character thoughts which provided valuable information about the main character. In Kennedy, dialogue is used in a similar way. When asked about their first day of school, Ben and Jamie respond:
“It was okay,” Ben finally said. How would we even begin to describe Kennedy?... “It was fine,” I agreed. And that was that. (312)

This shows parts of their characters. I learned that they were just like any typical high school student and lie to their parents. I also am given an insight into how the characters are going to deal with the conflict, and that is on their own in a very passive way. I expected them to respond this way by not mentioning it, but still, both Ben and Jamie’s responses showed how they handle conflict. Also, the internal thought of ‘and that was that’ really closed that door in the story.
In my first short story, Do Dreams Really Come True?,  The main character has a similar relationship with his partner. After asked why he is getting rid of his bike he does this:
I quickly come up with a lie since I don’t want to worry her: “I found someone to buy it and I don’t really need it.”

This reveals that his character is anxious and that his relationship with his partner is strained. The dialogue in my story works in a similar manner to the dialogue in Kennedy, because the characters dialogue contradicts their thoughts.

In Kennedy, Ben and Jaimie continue to not mention being bullied by Kennedy to anyone and this helps bring the story along and gives a better sense of their characters. If they spoke up about being bullied, their parents may have notified the school and the entire story would have lost it’s main conflict. It also would change Ben and Jamie’s characters and relationships with their parents. It is effective how the lack of dialogue between the parents and the kids makes the conflict spiral out of control.
There is another moment in Kennedy, that relates well to my second story and that is through a clear argumentative conflict.

When we got to Kennedy’s house, he refused to get out of the car. “Come inside with me,” he kept saying – an insistent, monotonous refrain. “Come inside. Just come inside. Come inside and see something.”
“Please, Kennedy,” I said. “It’s late.
“We have homework,” Ben Said.
“We have homework,” I corroborated.
“Just come inside,” he said again. “Come inside and let me just show you this one thing. This one thing and then you can go. Come inside. Come inside my house.”(321).

Then they went in the house where the bullying finally became more twisted and violent. This argument marked the end of Ben and Jamie’s resistance to Kennedy. Kennedy’s refusal to let them leave was evident in his repetition of his demands, but also the monotone and cold way that Kennedy spoke showed a chilling side of Kennedy that was even darker than earlier imagined.

I really like the way Ben and Jamie’s characters act so similarly, which is evident throughout the story but especially clear by their identical responses to Kennedy’s demands and then simultaneous acceptance to go inside.

annotations.comment: Yes, agreed. It’s hard to pick apart whether they’re just extremely similar or influencing each other to be so.

paragraphs.body: My second story, The Ventana, follows two fishers who are desperate to catch a fish. Both of their characters are revealed through dialogue, and that is also how the conflict develops. Similarly, to in Kennedy, the antagonist in my story asks repeatedly to go to the Ventana, but the protagonist keeps arguing not too. Finally, he gives in and the dialogue shows how that conflict ended.

Diego made his final offer, “Look Manuel, I’ll take my chances. You don’t have to cast a rod, I’ll do all the fishing. If you don’t want to eat anything tonight that’s on you, but I’m not going home empty handed again. I’ll even steer the ship too.”
“You won’t let up will you,” Manuel responded, “I’m not letting you drive my boat, but I’ll drive you there. The boat stays out of it, and when I say it’s time to go, we go.”

Here, even though Manuel ultimately gives in to Diego, I can tell that he is a stronger character than Jamie and Ben because his defeat is met with a strong compromise that keeps him in control. In Kennedy, I was confused and wanted to yell at Ben and Jamie to not go inside and accept defeat, whereas with Manuel I didn’t feel the same way; his character feels more in control than Ben and Jamie. Ben and Jamie could have tried a different response to prevent themselves from losing complete control, but their acceptance that they were trapped in this was clear from their very first dialogue on the matter.

annotations.context_left: r character than Jamie and Ben because his defeat is met with a strong compromise that keeps him in
annotations.context_right: . In Kennedy, I was confused and wanted to yell at Ben and Jamie to not go inside and accept defeat,

paragraphs.body: Dialogue creates characters and drives conflict; but writing it is so difficult. Sometimes it sounds cliché, others, it sounds implausible; reading dialogue that flows is a satisfying experience. Both stories I have written have different uses of dialogue. In my first story, the dialogue is limited and for the second, I started listening to the advice in Anne Lamont’s “Bird by Bird” by paying closer attention to the dialogue in the stories that I read. While reading Kennedy, when I got to the part where Jamie and Ben say that they can’t go inside Kennedy’s home, it felt so raw and tense that I forgot I was reading. When they both lie to their parents, the dialogue felt so natural. In my stories, there is a real sense of tension in The Ventana, and contradiction in Do Dreams Really Come True? Dialogue is the driving force in both of these stories and without it, they wouldn’t function as well. It is essential to any good story and good use of it can make a bad story better.

annotations.comment: Agreed! Nice paper, Nathan, and it’s been fun to work with you this term. Thanks!
